title: Lesson 379
transcript: One of the things you'll notice about the exam outline and how ISE2 does things is that there's obviously overlap in, first of all, all of the domains that make up the common body of knowledge. of security and therefore the CISSP. So there's a lot of overlap in materials, although what we need to remember is that based on the domains themselves, that material that overlaps is not repeated. It's looked at from different perspectives. So here we are in domain number one, which is all about security management. So we're talking about the management aspects of security and how it has to be aligned with goals and objectives of the organization. This entire domain is focused on how security supports the organization and its initiatives. In other words, the business itself. Now, when we look at this next section here, It deals with a very important part of any organization, and that is preparing itself for disasters. Typically, it's what we call business continuity management. That is the accountability of any CEO and board of directors, for example, right? Because the CEO is obviously there to ensure that the organization achieves its goals and objectives, increases its value. But part of that means that it has to be able to survive and withstand any negative eventuality that we would refer as being a disaster. That is the accountability of a CEO. Now, obviously in domain one, we're focused on how security supports that. All of these different sections that we're going through are focused on how security supports a lot of these activities and so on. And that includes business continuity management. Now you may ask, why is security focused on business continuity management? And the simple answer to that is that you cannot prepare yourself for a disaster and be able to withstand it and survive it unless security has been involved right from the start and throughout. In fact, if you think about the words business continuity management, It's how we manage business continuity in the event of a disaster. And so that means that whatever plan, which is usually referred to as business continuity plans and disaster recovery plans, better have been written and created with security in mind. If an organization is going through a disaster, the requirements to protect its assets become even more important than they were before the disaster. And of course, it becomes really important for security to focus not only on protecting valuable assets as the organization is dealing with whatever disaster it is experiencing, But it's also important to protect people and relationships and anything that obviously may be at risk as a result of the disaster itself. And what's important to remember here is that we're not just focused on natural disasters. We can constitute a disaster as being anything that has the opportunity of putting the entire organization at risk. And so it's especially important during the disaster to protect all assets based on value which is what security is focused on to begin with. But it's also important to ensure that security has been involved so that the organization can do other things while it's dealing with the disaster itself and a perfect example of that is compliance. complying to laws and regulations and privacy requirements becomes even more important in the event of a disaster. I mean, an organization cannot use an excuse like, well, we're experiencing a disaster and therefore we can't meet the requirements of these privacy laws or data protection laws or maybe other requirements that it has related to compliance. In fact, it may be even more important in the event of a disaster. to show and prove compliance to very tough laws and regulations and protect things obviously related to corporate reputation and the relationships and the image that the organization has worked really hard to instill with its clients and customers and other stakeholders. And so this section is a perfect example of where a lot of material overlaps in the different domains. So we're going to introduce first of all what we're talking about in this section here and we are 1.7 as you can see. But there's always value in reflecting on the actual words that you see here because ultimately this is where the exam questions come from. So when people at ISE 2 start writing the exam questions that are focused on measuring your competence level. They actually look at these words here and write questions focused on being able to measure whether you're competent. in each of these specific areas that you see here based on the actual wording that you see here. So the way to interpret this is that as a competent security professional, in other words a CISSP, you need to be able to identify, analyze, assess, prioritize, and implement business continuity requirements. Now we're going to introduce just a couple of concepts here in this section because that's the way the exam outline is structured and then we'll talk in more detail about a lot of these things and what's required for security to participate in when we get to domain number seven, which is all about security operations, right? But let's focus on this first. And in fact, the first two things that come up in this section are related to what we consider to be a BIA, and external dependencies. Now, these are really important components of business continuity management for an organization. The ability to deal with and ensure business continuity in the event of a negative eventuality that we typically refer to as a disaster. So doing this business continuity is a really important corporate governance activity that ultimately the CEO is accountable for. but cannot be done properly unless security is involved properly. Now what we mean by a BIA, and we'll talk more about this when we get to domain number seven, but a BIA business impact assessment really is something that needs to involve the security function The security function has to be part of this BIA. If you focus on these words, business impact assessment, it's really focused on understanding what in the organization is truly critical and essential. A BIA is a way for an organization to understand what it has that's truly critical and essential as far as products and services and applications and relationships and assets and yes, systems and technologies, right? So this is a way to walk through your entire organization and understand what is truly critical and essential, but also, and pay attention to this, how critical and essential each of those things are, like for example, products, services, applications, systems, network segments. how critical and essential each of those things actually are based on time parameters. So the output of a BIA are gonna be time parameters, and you've heard of some of these, like the MTD, maximum tolerable downtime, which is also referred to as the MAD, maximum allowable downtime, sometimes in the industry. You've heard of the RTO, recovery time objective, and the RPO, recovery point objective, and WT, which is work recovery time, and so on. All of these we'll explain later in domain number seven in much more detail. And external dependencies. is what it sounds like, right? Because there may be all kinds of really important critical functions and processes and services and so on that rely on, for example, external parties. And so if you want to protect yourself from a business continuity perspective so that you can withstand and survive disasters, you need to think about these dependencies that you have on third parties like your cloud provider, for example, or others that are part of your supply chain. or vendors and services that are provided by other third parties. You need to understand the dependencies that you have from an external perspective. and address those as part of business continuity management as well. And those that are specifically focused on security is what obviously the security function needs to focus on as well. All right. So the bottom line is that when it comes to business continuity management, this is a really important corporate activity that needs to involve security right from the start and throughout, especially related to being involved in conducting a BIA, which obviously allows us to understand what's critical and essential, but also understanding all of these external dependencies and interconnections that the organization may have and how those would be affected in the event of a disaster. that obviously we need to be able to withstand as part of business continuity management. And as I mentioned a few times already, we'll describe a lot of these things in a lot more detail, including, for example, these time parameters and what they actually mean in domain number seven.
